_purpose: Hidden history overlay for the MS Delta cluster (Washington, Bolivar, Sunflower, Leflore, Coahoma, Tunica, Tallahatchie, Humphreys counties). Documents events, systems, and patterns that explain record gaps, reframe ancestor narratives, or reveal stories the records do not tell directly. The Delta's hidden history is a litany of organized exploitation: the domestic slave trade that populated it, the sharecropping system that entrapped its labor force, the racial violence that enforced the hierarchy, the deliberate destruction of Black political power, and the mechanization that expelled the people who built it. The dignity mandate applies throughout.

hidden_events.description: On August 28, 1955, Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam abducted fourteen-year-old Emmett Till from his great-uncle Moses Wright's home near Money, Mississippi. Till, visiting from Chicago, had allegedly whistled at or spoken to Carolyn Bryant at her husband's grocery store four days earlier. Bryant and Milam beat Till, shot him in the head, and threw his body in the Tallahatchie River weighted with a seventy-five-pound cotton gin fan. His body was recovered three days later. His mother, Mamie Till-Mobley, insisted on an open-casket funeral in Chicago so the world could see what had been done to her child. Photographs of Till's mutilated body, published in Jet magazine and the Chicago Defender, galvanized national outrage. Bryant and Milam were tried at the Tallahatchie County courthouse in Sumner in September 1955 and acquitted by an all-white jury in sixty-seven minutes. They later confessed to the killing in a paid interview with Look magazine in 1956.
hidden_events.record_impact: Trial transcript at Tallahatchie County Circuit Court, Sumner. FBI case files at the National Archives. Jet magazine and Chicago Defender photographs and reporting. Moses Wright's testimony identifying the murderers is among the most courageous courtroom moments in American history — a Black man in the Delta pointing at white men and saying 'Thar he.' The confession in Look magazine is part of the public record.
hidden_events.narrative_reframing: The murder of Emmett Till was not an isolated act of violence — it was the Delta's racial system operating exactly as designed. A perceived breach of racial etiquette by a Black child resulted in murder, and the justice system ratified the murder with acquittal. Till's mother's decision to show the world what had been done — and the media's willingness to publish it — made Emmett Till's death the catalyst for the civil rights movement.
hidden_events.dignity_note: Emmett Till was fourteen years old. He was a child. Name him. Name his mother, Mamie Till-Mobley, whose courage in demanding an open casket changed American history. Name the murderers: Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam. Name Moses Wright, who stood in a Mississippi courtroom and identified the killers of his great-nephew knowing that doing so could cost him his life. The sixty-seven-minute acquittal was not justice — it was the Delta's racial system functioning as intended.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: Emmett Till was fourteen years old when Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam murdered him in {ancestor_name}'s county — and the all-white jury that acquitted his killers in sixty-seven minutes told every Black family in the Delta exactly what the system thought a Black child's life was worth.
hidden_events.source: Whitfield, Stephen J. A Death in the Delta: The Story of Emmett Till. New York: Free Press, 1988. Tyson, Timothy B. The Blood of Emmett Till. New York: Simon & Schuster, 2017.

hidden_events.event_name: Murder of Reverend George Washington Lee in Belzoni
hidden_events.description: On May 7, 1955, three months before Emmett Till's murder, Reverend George Washington Lee was shot in the face while driving his car in Belzoni, Humphreys County. Lee was an NAACP leader and one of the first Black people to register to vote in Humphreys County. He had been warned to remove his name from the voter rolls and had refused. Sheriff Ike Shelton claimed the lead pellets found in Lee's jaw were dental fillings. No one was charged with the murder. The FBI investigated but the case was closed without prosecution.
hidden_events.record_impact: FBI investigation files. NAACP records. Newspaper coverage (primarily Black press). The sheriff's refusal to classify the death as a homicide means there is no murder investigation file in county records — the absence of records is the story.
hidden_events.narrative_reframing: Reverend Lee's murder preceded Emmett Till's by three months but received far less national attention. The pattern was the same: a Black person exercising a constitutional right was murdered, and the local justice system refused to investigate. Lee's death demonstrates that the Delta's racial violence was systematic, not isolated.
hidden_events.dignity_note: Name Reverend George Washington Lee — minister, NAACP leader, voter, murder victim. Name Sheriff Ike Shelton, who called shotgun pellets dental fillings. The refusal to investigate was itself an act of violence.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: Reverend George Lee was shot dead in Belzoni for registering to vote — and the sheriff said the lead in his jaw was dental fillings, and the case was closed, and {ancestor_name} learned that in Humphreys County, voting could be a capital offense.
hidden_events.source: Dittmer, John. Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1994.

hidden_events.description: The Great Mississippi Flood of 1927 exposed the Delta's racial brutality in catastrophic conditions. At Greenville (Washington County), Black workers were forced to labor on the levee at gunpoint before it broke. After the Mounds Landing crevasse (April 21), thousands of Black refugees were stranded on the levee crown for weeks, controlled by armed white guards who prevented them from leaving — the planter class feared that if Black workers evacuated, they would never return. The Red Cross operated segregated camps. Black workers were forced to labor on relief operations under armed supervision. Reports of beatings, sexual assault, and denial of food to Black refugees emerged. The NAACP investigated and documented the abuses. Secretary of Commerce Herbert Hoover, who oversaw the federal relief effort, suppressed the reports to protect his presidential ambitions.
hidden_events.record_impact: Red Cross records, NAACP investigation files, newspaper coverage (especially Black press), and federal government records document the racial violence. Some records were suppressed. Oral histories from flood survivors provide accounts that official records do not capture.
hidden_events.narrative_reframing: The 1927 flood was not merely a natural disaster — it was a disaster that revealed the Delta's racial system in extremis. The armed guards, the forced labor, the segregated camps, and the denial of food to Black refugees were not aberrations — they were the system operating under emergency conditions, stripped of its pretenses.
hidden_events.dignity_note: The Black refugees on the Greenville levee were not passive victims — they were people held at gunpoint by their own government. The armed guards were local white men, many of them the same planters and overseers who controlled the sharecropping system. The forced labor was not 'volunteering' — it was coercion under threat of violence.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: When the levee broke at Mounds Landing, {ancestor_name} and thousands of other Black Delta residents were stranded on the levee crown — and the armed guards who kept them there proved that even in a flood, the Delta's system of control did not stop for water.
hidden_events.source: Barry, John M. Rising Tide: The Great Mississippi Flood of 1927 and How It Changed America. New York: Simon & Schuster, 1997.

hidden_events.description: Parchman Farm, the Mississippi State Penitentiary, is located in Sunflower County in the heart of the Delta. Built on 20,000 acres of Delta farmland in 1901, Parchman was designed as a plantation — the inmates (overwhelmingly Black) grew cotton under armed guard on the same Sharkey clay that surrounded the prison. The institution was a deliberate successor to slavery and convict leasing: Black men arrested under vagrancy laws, petty theft charges, and fabricated offenses were sentenced to years of forced agricultural labor. The conditions were brutal — armed guards on horseback (called 'trusty shooters,' they were inmates given weapons to guard other inmates), minimal food, no medical care, and punishments including whipping with a leather strap. Parchman was profitable — the prison generated revenue from cotton sales. The system fed on the surrounding counties: any Black man who fell afoul of the law, or who a planter wanted removed, could end up at Parchman.
hidden_events.record_impact: Parchman inmate records at MDAH document admissions, sentences, and (sometimes) deaths. County court records document the arrests and convictions that fed the system. Some records are incomplete because the prison was not subject to meaningful oversight.
hidden_events.narrative_reframing: Parchman Farm was not a prison in any modern sense — it was a state-owned plantation where forced labor produced cotton for profit. The people incarcerated there were overwhelmingly Black men convicted under laws designed to produce exactly this result. If an ancestor disappeared from Delta records — present in one census, absent in the next — Parchman is a possible explanation.
hidden_events.dignity_note: The people at Parchman were not criminals in any meaningful sense. They were Black men trapped in a system that used the criminal justice apparatus to produce forced labor. Frame Parchman as what it was: slavery by another name, operated by the state of Mississippi.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: Parchman Farm sat in the middle of {ancestor_name}'s county — 20,000 acres of Delta cotton worked by prisoners under armed guard, a state-owned plantation that proved the system of forced labor had not ended in 1865, just changed its paperwork.
hidden_events.source: Oshinsky, David M. 'Worse Than Slavery': Parchman Farm and the Ordeal of Jim Crow Justice. New York: Free Press, 1996.

hidden_events.event_name: Eviction of Fannie Lou Hamer for Voter Registration
hidden_events.description: On August 31, 1962, Fannie Lou Hamer, a timekeeper and sharecropper on the W. D. Marlow plantation near Ruleville, Sunflower County, attempted to register to vote. She was immediately evicted from the plantation where she had lived and worked for eighteen years. The following night, night riders fired sixteen shots into the home of the family sheltering her. Hamer's subsequent organizing — including the formation of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and her testimony at the 1964 Democratic National Convention — made her a national figure. Her words, 'I'm sick and tired of being sick and tired,' became a rallying cry.
hidden_events.record_impact: SNCC organizational records. FBI surveillance files. Television footage of Hamer's 1964 DNC testimony. Sunflower County voter registration records document the systematic exclusion of Black voters.
hidden_events.narrative_reframing: Hamer's eviction demonstrated that in the Delta, exercising a constitutional right could cost a person their home, their livelihood, and their safety. Hamer was not an 'outside agitator' — she was a forty-four-year-old sharecropper who had picked cotton since age six and decided she had a right to vote.
hidden_events.dignity_note: Fannie Lou Hamer was a sharecropper, a mother, and a community leader who risked everything for the right to vote. Her courage was extraordinary — and it was shared by thousands of unnamed Black Mississippians who attempted to register and faced similar consequences.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: Fannie Lou Hamer picked cotton in {ancestor_name}'s county for eighteen years — and on the day she tried to register to vote, she was told to withdraw her registration or leave the plantation, and she chose to leave, and her leaving changed America.
hidden_events.source: Mills, Kay. This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer. New York: Dutton, 1993.

hidden_events.description: In the summer of 1964, the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) — a coalition of SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and the NAACP — organized Freedom Summer, a massive voter registration and education campaign across Mississippi. Hundreds of volunteers, including northern college students, came to Mississippi. The Delta was a primary target. Freedom Schools taught literacy and civics. Voter registration drives challenged the systematic exclusion of Black voters. The white power structure responded with violence: the murders of James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner in Neshoba County (June 21, 1964); the bombing of Black churches; the shooting of homes; arrests and beatings of volunteers and local activists. The Delta counties experienced coordinated economic retaliation — Black families who participated were evicted, fired, and denied credit.
hidden_events.record_impact: COFO/SNCC organizational records at the Smithsonian. FBI investigation files. Freedom School records. Arrest records from Delta counties. The documentary evidence is extensive because Freedom Summer was deliberately documented by its organizers.
hidden_events.narrative_reframing: Freedom Summer was not imposed on the Delta from outside — it was organized in partnership with local Black leaders who had been fighting for decades. The volunteers from the North provided visibility and legal support, but the courage came from local people who had to live in the Delta after the volunteers went home.
hidden_events.dignity_note: Name the local people as well as the famous figures. The Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party delegation at the 1964 DNC — Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, Victoria Gray — were Delta people, not outside agitators. The volunteers who were beaten and jailed chose to come; the local people who housed them had no choice but to stay.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: Freedom Summer 1964 came to {ancestor_name}'s county — and the question for every Black family was whether to participate, knowing that the consequences could be eviction, job loss, violence, or death, and knowing that not participating meant accepting the system as it was.
hidden_events.source: McAdam, Doug. Freedom Summer. New York: Oxford University Press, 1988. Dittmer, Local People (1994).

systemic_erasures.system_name: Systematic Exclusion of Enslaved People from Individual Records
systemic_erasures.population_affected: Tens of thousands of enslaved people across the eight Delta counties
systemic_erasures.description: Enslaved people in the Delta were excluded from the individual-level records that document free people's lives. They do not appear in census records by name (only as tick marks in slaveholder schedules). They do not appear in marriage records. They appear in court records primarily as property — in estate inventories, bills of sale, and sheriff's sales. This exclusion was the legal architecture of a system that classified human beings as chattel property.
systemic_erasures.record_consequence: For enslaved ancestors in the Delta before 1865, the primary sources are: slaveholder census schedules (1850, 1860), estate inventories, bills of sale, plantation records (where they survive), and the Freedmen's Bureau records (1865-1872) that bridge to post-emancipation identity.
systemic_erasures.narrative_hook: Before 1865, {ancestor_name} existed in the records of {county} County only as a line in a slaveholder's inventory — age, sex, color, and a dollar value. The system that enslaved {pronoun_object} also erased {pronoun_object} from the written record.
systemic_erasures.source: Gutman, Herbert G. The Black Family in Slavery and Freedom, 1750-1925. New York: Pantheon Books, 1976.

systemic_erasures.population_affected: Majority of the Black population in all eight counties
systemic_erasures.description: The annual crop settlement — when the sharecropper's share was calculated against the year's advances — was systematically manipulated to keep families in debt. The planter controlled the gin, the scale, the grading, and the accounting books. Sharecroppers who could not read the settlement sheet had no way to verify the arithmetic. Those who challenged it risked eviction, blacklisting, or violence.
systemic_erasures.record_consequence: Plantation and merchant ledgers document the settlement system from the landowner's perspective. The sharecropper's experience is recorded in oral histories, Freedmen's Bureau complaints (1865-1872), and WPA interviews.
systemic_erasures.narrative_hook: The settlement sheet at the commissary was the one document that determined {ancestor_name}'s year — and the arithmetic always came out wrong in the same direction, because the person doing the arithmetic was the same person who set the prices, weighed the cotton, and owned the store.
systemic_erasures.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985). Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.

systemic_erasures.system_name: Voter Registration Exclusion (1890 Constitution and Beyond)
systemic_erasures.population_affected: Entire Black population of the eight Delta counties
systemic_erasures.description: The Mississippi Constitution of 1890 was written explicitly to disenfranchise Black voters through poll taxes, literacy tests, and 'understanding' clauses administered by white registrars. The convention president stated the goal openly. The result in the Delta was near-total: in counties that were 70-80% Black, the voter rolls were almost entirely white. Leflore County had zero registered Black voters for decades. The system was maintained through economic retaliation (eviction, firing), physical violence, and legal intimidation.
systemic_erasures.record_consequence: Voter registration records document the exclusion — the rolls are white in counties that were overwhelmingly Black. Literacy test records show discriminatory administration. The absence of Black voters is the record.
systemic_erasures.narrative_hook: The Mississippi Constitution of 1890 was written to keep {ancestor_name} from voting — and it worked. In a county where Black families outnumbered white families four to one, the voter rolls were white, the registrar's literacy test was a door that only opened one way, and the consequences of pushing were fatal.
systemic_erasures.source: McMillen, Dark Journey (1989). Dittmer, Local People (1994).

underground_networks.description: The Delta blues was not merely entertainment — it was an information network, a means of emotional expression, and a cultural resistance practice. Blues musicians traveled between plantations, carrying news, songs, and a sense of connection across isolated communities. The jook joint where blues was performed was the Black community's secular gathering space — the counterpart to the church. Blues lyrics encoded the reality of Delta life: the work, the debt, the violence, the desire to leave, and the love that survived despite everything. The music traveled the same route as the Great Migration — from Delta jook joints to Memphis's Beale Street to Chicago's South Side, where it was electrified and became the foundation of rock and roll.
underground_networks.record_traces: Recorded blues lyrics (Library of Congress field recordings by Alan Lomax, 1941-1942; commercial recordings from the 1920s forward). Oral histories. Music venue records (where they exist). The recordings themselves are primary documents.
underground_networks.narrative_hook: The blues that traveled through {ancestor_name}'s community was not just music — it was news, it was testimony, it was the sound of people telling the truth about the Delta in a form that the planter class could hear but not suppress.
underground_networks.source: Lomax, Alan. The Land Where the Blues Began. New York: Pantheon Books, 1993. Palmer, Robert. Deep Blues. New York: Viking, 1981.

underground_networks.description: The voter registration campaigns in the Delta operated through the Black church network. SNCC and COFO organizers worked with local leaders — ministers, teachers, landowners — to build community-level organizing capacity. The organizational structure was cellular: church congregations served as units, mass meetings as decision-making bodies, and freedom songs as solidarity tools. The white power structure responded with surveillance, economic retaliation, and violence. Local people who housed civil rights workers risked their homes and lives.
underground_networks.record_traces: SNCC/COFO organizational records at the Smithsonian and University of Southern Mississippi. FBI surveillance files. Arrest records. Church meeting minutes. Mass meeting recordings. Freedom School records.
underground_networks.narrative_hook: Before the national media arrived, {ancestor_name}'s church was already the meeting place — where the community gathered to discuss voter registration, where freedom songs were rehearsed, and where the decision to risk everything was made collectively.
underground_networks.source: Payne, Charles M. I've Got the Light of Freedom. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1995. Dittmer, Local People (1994).

reframing_narratives.assumption: The Delta was 'empty wilderness' when American planters arrived.
reframing_narratives.reality: The Delta was Choctaw and Chickasaw homeland. The Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830) forced Choctaw cession. The 'wilderness' was deliberately cleared by enslaved labor — the forest was not empty; it was taken, and the people who cleared it were forced to do so.
reframing_narratives.source: Cobb, The Most Southern Place on Earth (1992).

reframing_narratives.assumption: The sharecropping system was a fair labor arrangement.
reframing_narratives.reality: Sharecropping was designed to replicate slavery's labor extraction under nominally free conditions. The planter controlled the land, the tools, the gin, the grading, the weighing, the credit, and the accounting. The system was mathematically rigged — the sharecropper's share was calculated by the planter, charged against advances set by the planter, and settled in a reckoning the planter controlled. Freedom to leave was often theoretical: debt peonage laws, vagrancy statutes, and physical violence kept families on the land.
reframing_narratives.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985). Wright, Old South, New South (1986).

reframing_narratives.assumption: The Great Migration was about seeking 'better opportunities.'
reframing_narratives.reality: The Great Migration from the Delta was about escaping a specific system — sharecropping, disenfranchisement, racial violence, and the complete absence of legal protection. Delta families did not leave for 'opportunity' in the abstract — they left because the Delta offered nothing: no vote, no fair settlement, no safety, and eventually no work when the machines came.
reframing_narratives.source: Wilkerson, The Warmth of Other Suns (2010).

reframing_narratives.reality: The blues was testimony. It documented the reality of Delta life in the only form available to a population that was excluded from the written record. Blues lyrics are primary sources — they describe working conditions, economic exploitation, racial violence, migration, and emotional life with a specificity that no other source provides.
reframing_narratives.source: Lomax, The Land Where the Blues Began (1993).

record_gap_explanations.explanation: Mississippi did not require statewide birth and death registration until 1912, and compliance was poor in rural Delta counties until the 1920s-1930s. Black births and deaths were significantly underregistered even after 1912.
record_gap_explanations.workaround: Federal census records (1830-1940). Freedmen's Bureau records (1865-1872). Church records. Plantation records. Funeral home records (Black funeral homes in Delta towns maintained detailed records).
record_gap_explanations.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History.

record_gap_explanations.explanation: The enslaved population (majority of the Delta) appears in antebellum records only as property — unnamed in census slaveholder schedules, listed by first name only in estate inventories. No marriage, birth, or death records exist for enslaved people.
record_gap_explanations.workaround: Slaveholder census schedules (1850, 1860) cross-referenced with estate inventories. Freedmen's Bureau records as the bridge to post-emancipation identity. Plantation manuscript collections at MDAH and university special collections.
record_gap_explanations.source: Gutman, The Black Family in Slavery and Freedom (1976).

record_gap_explanations.explanation: Sharecropper families moved frequently — 30-50% annual turnover between plantations. A family present in one county at one census may be in an adjacent county at the next, or may have left the Delta entirely. The constant movement makes census matching extremely difficult.
record_gap_explanations.workaround: Search adjacent counties in each census year. Use church records for continuity evidence. Plantation records (where they survive) document specific families by year.

dignity_notes.guidance: Name enslaved individuals wherever records permit. Use 'enslaved person,' not 'slave' as a noun. The domestic slave trade that populated the Delta was human trafficking — families shattered, people marched and shipped in chains. Do not use passive language.

Emmett Till was a child — fourteen years old. Name him. Name his mother, Mamie Till-Mobley. Name the murderers: Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam. Name Moses Wright, who identified them. The acquittal was not justice — it was the system protecting its own.

Name the victims. Name the killers where known. Lynching was organized racial terrorism, not spontaneous violence. Do not use passive voice. The purpose was the maintenance of white supremacy through spectacular violence.

Parchman was slavery by another name — a state-owned plantation where forced labor produced cotton for profit. The people incarcerated there were overwhelmingly Black men arrested under laws designed to produce forced labor, not to enforce justice.

Sharecroppers were not failed farmers — they were people trapped in a system designed to extract labor without providing a living. The arithmetic of the crop-lien system was the weapon. Frame sharecropping as an economic system imposed on a population without alternatives.

The removal of Native peoples from the Delta was ethnic cleansing by treaty and military force. Do not use 'they moved' — they were forced from their homeland. The land they lost was immediately converted to cotton plantation worked by enslaved labor.

Distinguish between local leaders (who had to stay and face consequences) and outside volunteers (who could leave). Both were courageous, but the local people risked more. Name local leaders: Fannie Lou Hamer, Amzie Moore, Aaron Henry, Robert Burns.

narrative_hooks: The Delta's hidden history is a story of systems — the domestic slave trade that populated it, the sharecropping system that exploited it, the convict leasing system that re-enslaved it, and the mechanization that expelled it. {ancestor_name} lived inside these systems, and the records they left behind reflect the systems' purposes, not the people's humanity.

Emmett Till's murder in {ancestor_name}'s region was not an aberration — it was the system functioning as designed, and the sixty-seven-minute acquittal was the system confirming that it had functioned correctly.

Parchman Farm sat in the heart of the Delta like a confession — a state-owned plantation where forced labor produced cotton, proving that the system of extraction had not ended in 1865, just changed its legal basis.

The settlement sheet at the commissary was the Delta's most effective instrument of control — {ancestor_name} worked the cotton, but the planter controlled the arithmetic, and the arithmetic always produced debt.

Before 1865, {ancestor_name} existed in {county} County records only as a line in a property inventory — the system that enslaved {pronoun_object} also erased {pronoun_object} from the written record, and the genealogist's challenge is to find the person behind the inventory number.

Fannie Lou Hamer was sick and tired of being sick and tired — and {ancestor_name}'s community, which had been sick and tired for a hundred years, recognized in her voice the sound of their own exhaustion finally finding words.
source_references: Whitfield, Stephen J. A Death in the Delta: The Story of Emmett Till. New York: Free Press, 1988.
Tyson, Timothy B. The Blood of Emmett Till. New York: Simon & Schuster, 2017.
Barry, John M. Rising Tide: The Great Mississippi Flood of 1927 and How It Changed America. New York: Simon & Schuster, 1997.
Oshinsky, David M. 'Worse Than Slavery': Parchman Farm and the Ordeal of Jim Crow Justice. New York: Free Press, 1996.
Mills, Kay. This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer. New York: Dutton, 1993.
Payne, Charles M. I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1995.
Dittmer, John. Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1994.
McAdam, Doug. Freedom Summer. New York: Oxford University Press, 1988.
Cobb, James C. The Most Southern Place on Earth: The Mississippi Delta and the Roots of Regional Identity. New York: Oxford University Press, 1992.
McMillen, Neil R. Dark Journey: Black Mississippians in the Age of Jim Crow. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1989.
Lomax, Alan. The Land Where the Blues Began. New York: Pantheon Books, 1993.
Gutman, Herbert G. The Black Family in Slavery and Freedom, 1750-1925. New York: Pantheon Books, 1976.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
